fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get this serializer toolkit wrapped up. The core idea is that API consumers shouldn't have to get back giant payloads when they only need a couple of fields, and we also want to avoid accidentally leaking internal database IDs in URLs or responses. Think of it like what we discussed back when we built the sparse fields thing for the dashboard — same philosophy but more robust and covering nested relationships too.

Right now the pain points we're hearing from the front-end team are: (1) responses are too fat by default and they have to strip data client-side, (2) when they POST related records they're not sure if the identifier they sent was actually valid or just silently dropped, and (3) the URLs we expose contain raw numeric IDs which the security team flagged.

We also need it to handle cases where someone asks for a field that doesn't exist — right now it just silently ignores it and the front-end has no idea their query param was a typo. Same for when someone asks for both a parent object and one of its children at the same time, that should be an error too.

There's also the request context wiring — query params like `only`, `exclude`, `expand` etc. should flow through automatically, but serializer-level attribute defaults should act as fallbacks when no query params are present (check how we handled the override priority in that earlier PR). Status codes and lookup tokens need to survive through the API response wrapper as well.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def serialize(self, fields=None, exclude=None, expand=None, expand_id_only=None, validate_expand_instructions=True) -> dict

trigger_keywords: what happens when no fields are specified
default output when fields list is empty or omitted
behavior with missing fields parameter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the `fields` parameter is omitted or is an empty list for both select_fields and exclude_fields operations, the complete nested product representation must be emitted in full, including all nested objects and collections. No pruning occurs.

trigger_keywords: field path separator
how to address nested fields
double underscore path syntax
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Nested field paths must use double-underscore (`__`) as the separator, e.g., `manufacturer__name` or `skus__owners__organization`. This applies consistently to select_fields, exclude_fields, expand, and expand_id_only operations.

trigger_keywords: requesting both parent object and child field
selecting whole object and one of its descendants
ambiguous selection of object and subfield
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a selection set includes both a whole nested object (e.g., `manufacturer`) and a descendant of that same object (e.g., `manufacturer__name`), the system must return `{"error":"invalid_field_selection"}`. This is distinct from an unknown field error.

trigger_keywords: unknown field path error
typo in field name
non-existent field in selection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If any field path in a select_fields or exclude_fields request does not correspond to an actual field at the addressed level in the nested representation, the system must return `{"error":"unknown_field"}`. Error output must be a normalized JSON object and must not expose host-language exception names or stack traces.

trigger_keywords: expand depth limit
how deep can expansion go
maximum nesting for expand paths
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Expansion paths are limited to a maximum depth of 3 segments (e.g., `cars__model__manufacturer` is valid but `cars__model__manufacturer__models` exceeds the limit). Exceeding the depth returns `{"error":"expand_depth_exceeded"}`.

trigger_keywords: expand_id_only on non-collection field
identifier-only expansion on singular relation
when is expand_id_only invalid
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: `expand_id_only` is only valid for collection-type relation leaves. Applying it to a singular relation (e.g., `organization`, which is a single object relation) must return `{"error":"invalid_field_selection"}`.

trigger_keywords: disable expansion validation
skip unknown field errors in expand
validate_expand_instructions false behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When `validate_expand_instructions` is set to `false`, unknown expansion paths are silently ignored rather than returning an error. Valid expansion instructions in the same request still apply normally and produce their expected expanded output.

trigger_keywords: read-only relation identifier in write payload
relation id ignored during validation
manufacturer_id dropped from validated data
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In `read_only_relation` profile mode for validate_write, relation identifier fields (e.g., `manufacturer_id`) present in the input data are silently ignored and do not appear in `validated_data`. Only writable scalar fields are returned.

trigger_keywords: writable relation resolution
resolving relation id to record summary
validated_data structure for writable relation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In `writable_relation` profile mode for validate_write, the relation identifier is retained in `validated_data` and additionally resolved to a `{record_type, id, name}` summary under a `{field}_resolved` key. If the required relation identifier is absent, the response is `{"errors":{"manufacturer_id":["This field is required."]},"is_valid":false}`.

trigger_keywords: external identifier encoding
opaque ID for internal numeric ID
hashid or obfuscated identifier
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Internal numeric identifiers must be encoded into short opaque external identifiers (e.g., `1` → `"4Nt1"`). The system must support `encode_field` (returns just the external_id), `encode_record` (returns full record with id replaced by external id), and `decode_field` (converts external_id back to internal_id). Invalid external identifiers return `{"error":"invalid_external_id"}`.

trigger_keywords: hyperlink with external id in URL
external identifier in relation URL
null link for unsaved record
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Relation hyperlinks must embed the external (opaque) identifier in the URL rather than the internal numeric ID (e.g., `http://testserver/models/AMTw/`). For unsaved records that have no persisted identity, the link field must return `null`.

trigger_keywords: request query param priority over attribute defaults
override behavior for only exclude expand params
when do serializer attribute defaults apply
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When query parameters (`only`, `exclude`, `expand`, `expand_id_only`) are present in the request, they take priority and the serializer-level attribute defaults (`attribute_only`, `attribute_exclude`, `attribute_expand`, `attribute_expand_id_only`) are ignored. The attribute defaults only apply when the corresponding query parameter is absent from the request.

trigger_keywords: comma-separated query string parsing
string vs array for query params
how expand and only are parsed from request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Query parameters may be supplied as either a JSON array or as a comma-separated string. Both `"expand": "a,a1"` and `"expand": ["a", "a1"]` must produce identical results. The context builder normalizes both forms into lists.

trigger_keywords: api response wrapper status code
lookup token preserved in response
external lookup not found response
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The API response wrapper must preserve the `status_code` alongside the serialized `data`. For external lookup operations, the opaque `lookup` token must appear at the top level of the response alongside `data` and `status_code`. When the looked-up record does not exist, status_code must be 404 and data must be `{"detail":"Not found."}`.

trigger_keywords: child serializer hierarchy path
nested serializer path tracking
context_label passed to child serializer
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each child serializer must track its position in the nested hierarchy as a double-underscore-joined path string (e.g., `"skus__owners__organization"`). The root serializer has an empty string hierarchy. When a calculated child is rendered, it receives its parent's context including a `context_label` and reflects its own `hierarchy` path as its key name.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: check how we handled the override priority in that earlier PR
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The request context building logic in feature 11 (feature11_build_serializer_context_from_request.json): when query params are present they override serializer attribute-level defaults; attribute defaults are only used as fallback when query params are absent. See the last two test cases in that file.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the sparse fields thing for the dashboard — same philosophy but more robust
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Features 1–4 (select_fields and exclude_fields operations) covering field path selection/exclusion with double-underscore nested paths, unknown_field errors, and invalid_field_selection errors for ambiguous parent+child selections.

The external identifier encoding/decoding logic in feature 8 (feature8_external_identifier_fields.json) and feature 9 (feature9_external_identifier_hyperlinks.json), specifically the hashid-style opaque ID scheme used for both field-level encoding and hyperlink URL construction.

context_pointers.vague_reference: status codes and lookup tokens need to survive through the API response wrapper
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 12 (feature12_apply_extensions_in_api_responses.json): the api_response and external_lookup operations must include status_code and, for external lookups, the opaque lookup token at the top level of the JSON response object.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented here. Could you check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature, or look at the PRD section in start.md that covers that operation? The exact expected input/output behavior is defined there.

feature10_child_serializer_context.json.description: Track nested serializer path names and preserve parent context when rendering a calculated child serializer.

feature11_build_serializer_context_from_.description: Build serializer context from view defaults and HTTP query parameters, including comma-delimited parameters and override behavior.

feature12_apply_extensions_in_api_respon.description: Apply request-supplied extension instructions through an API view response and preserve framework-visible response signals.

feature1_select_requested_fields.json.description: Serialize a nested product record while keeping only the requested root or nested fields.

feature2_reject_invalid_field_selection..description: Reject requested field paths that do not correspond to fields in the nested representation or that ambiguously request both a whole object and one of its children.

feature3_exclude_requested_fields.json.description: Serialize a nested product record while removing the requested root or nested fields.

feature4_reject_invalid_exclusion_paths..description: Reject excluded field paths that do not correspond to fields in the nested representation.

feature5_expand_related_resources.json.description: Serialize a base owner record while expanding requested related resources fully, as identifiers only for collection leaves, or as additional same-record information.

feature6_validate_expansion_instructions.description: Validate expansion paths and expansion depth unless validation is explicitly disabled.

feature7_deserialize_expandable_relation.description: Validate write input for expandable relations, leaving read-only relation identifiers out of validated data and resolving writable relation identifiers to their target records.

feature8_external_identifier_fields.json.description: Convert internal numeric identifiers to opaque external identifiers and convert matching external identifiers back to internal identifiers.

feature9_external_identifier_hyperlinks..description: Render relation hyperlinks with the external identifier in the routed URL and return a null link for unsaved records.
